message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background and rationale</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Limited data on the efficacy and safety of currently applied COVID-19 therapeutics and their impact on COVID-19 outcomes have raised additional concern.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We estimated the impact of the current treatments on the efficacy and safety of COVID-19 by a meta-analysis. The comprehensive search included studies reporting clinical features and treatment strategies published from January 21, 2020, to May 15, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We included 52 studies that involved 13,966 COVID-19 patients. We found that the most prevalent treatments were antivirals (proportion: 0.74, 95% CI
                    <jats:sup>1</jats:sup>
                    : [0.65, 0.83]) and antibiotics (proportion: 0.73, 95% CI: [0.62, 0.83]). The COVID-19 severity increased among patients taking glucocorticoids (risk ratio (RR)
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    = 1.71, 95% CI: [1.06, 2.76]) or immunoglobulins (RR = 3.83, 95% CI: [1.27, 11.53]), and renal replacement therapy (RRT) and glucocorticoids increased the length of ICU stay (RRT
                    <jats:sup>3</jats:sup>
                    : RR = 11.89, 95% CI: [3.26, 43.39]; glucocorticoids: RR = 3.10, 95% CI: [1.52, 6.29]). The COVID-19 severity and mortality increased among patients taking tocilizumab (severity: F = 25.53,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.02; mortality: F
                    <jats:sup>4</jats:sup>
                    = 19.37,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.02). The most effective treatment was the combination of arbidol with lopinavir/ritonavir compared with placebo (mean difference = 0.5, 95% CI [-0.60, 1.66]), and the safest combination was remdesivir and lopinavir/ritonavir (RR = 0.78, 95% CI [0.32, 1.91]).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>glucocorticoids, immunoglobulins, RRT, and tocilizumab might worsen COVID-19 outcomes, and themost effective and safest treatment strategy for COVID-19 is the combination of different antivirals.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Evaluation of the current therapeutic approaches for COVID-19: a meta-analysis
